case_name: COMPAS Recidivism Risk Scoring Tool
source_quality_rationale: Primary investigative journalism with published methodology; Wisconsin Supreme Court filings; peer-reviewed academic literature
deployer: Multiple US state courts and county probation departments
affected_population: Criminal defendants subject to pre-trial detention, sentencing, and parole decisions
estimated_scale: Hundreds of thousands of defendants per year across multiple US states
scale_basis: ProPublica Broward County dataset; vendor commercial distribution
annex_iii_subcategory: Recidivism risk assessment for criminal justice
annex_mapping_rationale: Directly maps to Annex III law enforcement: automated recidivism scoring used in US criminal sentencing and parole
eu_scope_note: US deployment; Annex III mapping is analytical reference only
decision_criticality_rationale: Scores influence pre-trial detention and sentencing decisions affecting liberty and livelihood
autonomy_rationale: Scores materially influence judicial decisions; judges formally retain authority but documented high deference rates
vulnerability_rationale: Criminal defendants including pre-trial detainees; incarceration context constitutes heightened vulnerability
oversight_rationale: Judges nominally review; no documented requirement to justify deviation; high deference observed in ProPublica analysis
recourse_rationale: Algorithm was proprietary trade secret; courts rejected disclosure; defendants could not challenge methodology
scale_rationale: Commercial deployment across multiple US states; hundreds of thousands of decisions annually
opacity_rationale: Northpointe refused to disclose algorithm; trade secret protection upheld; no public documentation of features or weights
monitoring_rationale: No systematic monitoring; racial disparity in false positive rates discovered by ProPublica investigative journalism 2016
public_record_excerpt: ProPublica found COMPAS was nearly twice as likely to falsely flag Black defendants as future criminals compared to white defendants with equal reoffending rates. The vendor declined to disclose the algorithm. Courts across more than a dozen US states used COMPAS scores in sentencing.
coding_notes: Subject of Wisconsin Supreme Court State v Loomis 2016 which upheld use despite opacity; ProPublica vs Northpointe methodology dispute fully documented in public record
ambiguity_flags: Autonomy score contested between formal judicial authority and documented de-facto deference; exact national deployment scale not officially published

case_name: Australian Robodebt Welfare Debt Recovery
source_quality_rationale: Royal Commission with full published report; Senate inquiry; multiple court proceedings; extensive investigative journalism
affected_population: Welfare benefit recipients and former recipients
estimated_scale: Approximately 900000 debt notices issued 2016-2019
scale_basis: Department of Human Services statistics; Royal Commission report 2023
annex_iii_subcategory: Automated welfare debt recovery and overpayment assessment
annex_mapping_rationale: Directly maps to Annex III essential services: automated eligibility and debt determination affecting welfare access
eu_scope_note: Australian deployment; Annex III mapping is analytical reference only
decision_criticality_rationale: Automated debt demands caused severe financial distress; multiple deaths by suicide attributed to programme stress
autonomy_rationale: Fully automated income averaging with no individual human review of debt calculations before issuance
vulnerability_rationale: Welfare recipients including unemployed and low-income Australians; limited institutional resources to contest
oversight_rationale: No individual case review before debt notice; errors identified only when recipients contested
recourse_rationale: Onus reversed to recipients to disprove income average; practically unavailable for those with historical records destroyed
scale_rationale: National deployment across Australian welfare system; approximately 900000 debt notices issued
opacity_rationale: Income averaging methodology not disclosed in notices; debt calculation basis not explained to recipients
monitoring_rationale: No post-deployment monitoring; systematic flaws discovered via ABC journalism and individual complaints; Royal Commission confirmed unlawfulness
public_record_excerpt: The Australian Royal Commission found the Robodebt scheme automatically generated welfare debt notices by averaging ATO income data against Centrelink records without individual review. Approximately 900000 notices were issued. The Commission found the scheme was unlawful from inception and caused significant harm including deaths.
coding_notes: Royal Commission 2022-2023 found scheme was not a lawful debt; government paid approximately AUD 1.8 billion in settlements; Prime Minister apologised
ambiguity_flags: Exact number of deaths attributable to scheme disputed; Royal Commission declined to publish full attribution findings for individual cases

case_name: UK Ofqual A-Level Calculated Grades Algorithm 2020
source_quality_rationale: Government and Ofqual publications; subsequent parliamentary inquiry; substantial investigative journalism
deployer: Ofqual (Office of Qualifications and Examinations Regulation)
affected_population: UK A-level and GCSE students in summer 2020
estimated_scale: Approximately 700000 A-level and 1.5 million GCSE entries
annex_iii_subcategory: Automated grade standardisation replacing teacher-assessed grades for university access
annex_mapping_rationale: Directly maps to Annex III education: automated system determining grades for university access decisions
eu_scope_note: UK deployment; pre-dates UK AI Act; Annex III mapping is analytical reference only
decision_criticality_rationale: Grades determined university access; life-altering educational consequences for affected cohort
autonomy_rationale: Fully automated grade calculation replacing teacher predictions with no individual student case review
vulnerability_rationale: Students in asymmetric institutional context with limited ability to contest automated grades
oversight_rationale: No individual oversight of algorithm outputs; applied historical school-level data overriding teacher assessments
recourse_rationale: Appeals mechanism existed but initially accepted only centre-assessed grades; process reversed after public outcry
scale_rationale: National deployment affecting all UK A-level students in single cohort; approximately 700000
opacity_rationale: Standardisation model not publicly disclosed at time of results; specification released only after controversy
monitoring_rationale: No monitoring; severe downgrading of disadvantaged students discovered on results day; algorithm withdrawn after 9 days
public_record_excerpt: Ofqual's standardisation algorithm downgraded 39 per cent of teacher-predicted A-level grades. Students from schools with historically lower performance were disproportionately affected. The algorithm was withdrawn 9 days after results were published and teacher-predicted grades were restored.
coding_notes: Algorithm withdrawn and teacher grades restored; Ofqual and Secretary of State apologised; parliamentary inquiry conducted 2020
ambiguity_flags: Whether algorithm disproportionately affected state versus private school students by design or as emergent effect remained technically contested

ambiguity_flags: Extent of actual use in live hiring decisions is disputed; Reuters states it was not used in live evaluation; internal testing only confirmed

case_name: SyRI Netherlands Welfare Fraud Detection
source_quality_rationale: Court ruling with full legal reasoning; Dutch government legislative history; Amnesty investigation; legal aid proceedings
deployer: Dutch Ministry of Social Affairs and Employment
affected_population: Residents of designated high-risk postcode areas; disproportionately low-income and migrant communities
estimated_scale: Population of targeted municipalities covering hundreds of thousands of residents
scale_basis: Dutch government SyRI deployment records; parliamentary documentation
annex_iii_subcategory: Automated welfare fraud risk profiling of entire postcode populations
annex_mapping_rationale: Directly maps to Annex III essential services: automated risk scoring affecting welfare benefits and social service access
eu_scope_note: EU member state deployment; struck down by Hague District Court as violating ECHR Art.8; cited in EU AI Act debates
decision_criticality_rationale: Fraud flags lead to benefit investigations and suspension; significant welfare and livelihood consequences
autonomy_rationale: Automated risk profiling with de-facto determinative effect on who was investigated
vulnerability_rationale: Welfare recipients and residents in targeted postcodes including migrants and low-income households
oversight_rationale: No individual review of risk scores before investigation initiation; subjects not informed of their score
recourse_rationale: Targets not informed they were scored; no mechanism to challenge or access score

monitoring_rationale: No monitoring; legal challenge by Privacy First Foundation exposed methodology; court ordered discontinuation 2020
public_record_excerpt: The Hague District Court struck down SyRI in February 2020 finding it violated Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights. The court found the government had not demonstrated necessity and that the opacity made meaningful challenge impossible. Residents in targeted postcodes were scored without their knowledge.
coding_notes: Landmark ECHR Art.8 decision; first court finding that a welfare fraud AI system violated fundamental rights; preceded EU AI Act debates
ambiguity_flags: Exact algorithm features and thresholds never published; extent of decisions made on basis of SyRI scores not fully quantifiable

case_name: PredPol Predictive Policing Los Angeles
source_quality_rationale: Journalism with documented LAPD statements; LAPD audit report; academic studies; ACLU analysis

estimated_scale: Multiple city-wide deployments; Los Angeles covered approximately 20 patrol divisions

eu_scope_note: US deployment; Annex III mapping is analytical reference only

autonomy_rationale: Algorithm informs patrol deployment decisions; officers retain formal discretion over individual interactions
vulnerability_rationale: Residents in targeted areas; documented disparate impact on Black and Latino communities
oversight_rationale: Officers nominally retain discretion; no formal oversight of the algorithmic targeting itself

opacity_rationale: Proprietary PredPol model; algorithm and training data not publicly disclosed
monitoring_rationale: LAPD audit found no crime reduction benefit; self-reinforcing feedback loop documented; system discontinued 2020
public_record_excerpt: Los Angeles Police Department deployed PredPol from 2011 to direct patrol resources to algorithmically predicted hot spots. An internal LAPD audit found no evidence of crime reduction. Critics documented a self-reinforcing feedback loop where over-policed areas generated more arrest data feeding the model.
coding_notes: LAPD discontinued PredPol in 2020 following community opposition and lack of effectiveness evidence; Santa Cruz became first US city to ban predictive policing 2020
ambiguity_flags: Self-reinforcing feedback loop well documented but extent to which PredPol versus other policing practices caused disparate arrest rates is contested

case_name: IBM Watson for Oncology Treatment Recommendations

case_name: Detroit Police Facial Recognition False Arrest
source_quality_rationale: ACLU documentation of Robert Williams case; court records; NYT investigation; ACLU lawsuit documentation

estimated_scale: Detroit police jurisdiction; several hundred identification requests per year

vulnerability_rationale: Criminal suspects; documented higher false positive rates for Black individuals; incarceration risk
oversight_rationale: Detective reviews match but limited technical capacity to challenge output; Robert Williams case demonstrates inadequate review

Williams was held for 30 hours before release. The ACLU documented this as the first known wrongful arrest by facial recognition in the United States.
coding_notes: ACLU filed lawsuit; Detroit PD subsequently adopted use policies; NIST audit data showing higher error rates for dark-skinned individuals supports accuracy concerns
ambiguity_flags: Exact false positive rates of the specific DPD system not publicly disclosed; only the Williams case is publicly documented as wrongful arrest

case_name: Optum Patient Risk Algorithm Racial Bias
source_quality_rationale: Peer-reviewed Science publication by Obermeyer et al.; NYT reporting; Optum public response; academic follow-up studies

affected_population: Hospital patients enrolled in care management programs; Black patients disproportionately underserved

annex_iii_subcategory: Automated patient risk scoring for health care management program allocation

eu_scope_note: US deployment; Annex III mapping is analytical reference only
decision_criticality_rationale: Care management program allocation affects health outcomes; consequential for chronic disease management
autonomy_rationale: Automated scoring without individual review; score determines program eligibility
vulnerability_rationale: Patients with complex chronic conditions; documented significant underserving of Black patients
